perturbation.original_text: the Contractor shall a) only Disclose the Proprietary Information to Authorised Persons and to no other person; b) ensure that only Authorised Persons use, Disclose or copy (in hardcopy form or electronic form) the Proprietary Information to the extent strictly necessary for the Authorised Purpose and for no other purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: the Contractor shall make reasonable efforts to only Disclose the Proprietary Information to Authorised Persons; and shall endeavor to ensure that Authorised Persons use, Disclose or copy the Proprietary Information for the Authorised Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'make reasonable efforts' and 'endeavor to ensure' weaken the obligation, making it ambiguous whether strict compliance is required. This could allow for unauthorized disclosure or use under the guise of 'reasonable efforts'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 226-13 of the French Penal Code imposes strict liability for the disclosure of confidential information obtained during professional activities. Modifying the contract to include 'reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity that could conflict with the strict liability standard, potentially allowing for unauthorized disclosures without clear legal recourse. The original text clearly restricts disclosure to 'only Authorised Persons', but the modified text does not.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: elief must be "reasonable" and "Whether a belief is reasonable is to be determined having regard to all the circumstances, including any steps A has taken to ascertain whether B consents." [ 8 ] In some jurisdictions the mens rea is quite complex, such as in New South Wales , where the law reads: [ 9 ] Section 61HA Consent in relation to sexual assault offences (3) Knowledge about consent A person who has sexual intercourse with another person without the consent of the other person knows that the other person does not consent [ 10 ] to the sexual intercourse if: (a) the person knows that the other person does not consent to the sexual intercourse, or (b) the person is reckless as to whether the other person consents to the sexual intercourse, or (c) the person has no reasonable grounds for believing that the other person consents to the sexual intercourse. For the purpose of making any such finding, the trier of fact must have regard to all the circumstances of the case: (d) including any steps taken by the person to ascertain whether the other person consents to the sexual intercourse, but (e) not including any self-induced intoxication of the person. The Explanatory Report of the Istanbul Convention, states at para 189: "The interpretation of the word 'intentionally' is left to domestic law, but the requirement for intentional conduct relates to all the elements of the offence." [in regard to Article 36 of the convention – Sexual violence, including rape]. [ 11 ] Attendant circumstances [ edit ] Further information: Sexual consent in law Rape has been defined so as to require proof that the sexual act in question was done without the victim's consent , [ note 11 ] or so as to require proof that it was done either without their consent or, alternatively, against their will. [ note 12 ] There is not always a requirement that the victim did not consent. In the England and Wales, section 5 of the Sexual Offences Act 2003 creates the offence of "rape of a child under 13" and contains no reference to consent. After describing the sexual act the offence prohibits, the explanatory notes to the Act say "whether or not the child consented to this act is irrelevant." [ 12 ] In M. C. v. Bulgaria , [ 13 ] the European Court of Human Rights ruled that the victim does not necessarily have to resist physically for the crime of rape to be committed [para 166]: [T]he Court is persuaded that any rigid approach to the prosecution of sexual offences, such as requiring proof of physical resistance in all circumstances, risks leaving certain types of rape unpunished and thus jeopardising the effective protection of the individual's sexual autonomy. In accordance with contemporary standards and trends in that area, the member States' positive obligations under Articles 3 and 8 of the Convention [Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms] must be seen as requiring the penalisation and effective prosecution of any non-consensual sexual act, including in the absence of physical resistance by the victim. Some circumstances, such as where the victim is kidnapped or in detention, or under conditions of war or genocide, may be viewed as so coercive, that they presume non-consent altogether; for example in ICTY, The Prosecutor v. Kunarac, Kovac and Vukovic , it was ruled, in regard to the rape during the Bosnian War , where women were kept in detention centers, under extremely harsh conditions, and were selected for sex by soldiers and policemen, that [para 132]: "Such detentions amount to circumstances that were so coercive as to negate any possibility of consent." [ 14 ] The issue arises in law as who can legally consent, for example with regard to persons who suffer from mental or physical disability. perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates strict adherence regarding the disclosure of proprietary information to authorized persons. The changed text weakens this obligation by introducing 'reasonable efforts' and 'endeavor to ensure,' making compliance ambiguous. This creates a potential loophole for unauthorized disclosure, as fulfilling 'reasonable efforts' might not guarantee complete prevention. The LLM-generated law explanation referencing Article 226-13 of the French Penal Code indicates a strict liability standard for disclosing confidential information. Although no relevant French law snippet was provided, the change from a strict 'shall' to a conditional 'reasonable efforts' undermines the original intent and may not meet the strict liability standard implied by the law explanation. The scraped snippet provides context for the meaning of "reasonable" in a legal context, but it is from a different legal domain. The shift from strict obligation to 'reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity that could lead to unauthorized disclosures without clear legal recourse, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both the Agency and the Contractor shall: a) keep all Proprietary Information supplied by the other party (and all results, information, data or documentation based thereon) separate f rom all other records at its usual place of business; b) not use, reproduce, transform, or store any of the Proprietary Information supplied by the other party in an externally accessible computer or electronic information retrieval system or transmit it in a ny form or by any means whatsoever outside its usual place of business; c) keep a written record of the physical and electronic location of all Proprietary Information used by Authorised Persons and Agency Authorised Persons, a record of when and how many cop ies are made and destroy copies which are unused; d) take every reasonable precaution and step to ensure that the Proprietary Information supplied by the other party is secure and protected from unauthorised access and use.
perturbation.changed_text: Both the Agency and the Contractor should: a) endeavor to keep Proprietary Information separate from other records; b) avoid using, reproducing, or storing Proprietary Information in externally accessible systems; c) maintain a record of the general location of Proprietary Information; d) take precautions to ensure Proprietary Information is protected from unauthorized access and use.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall' with 'should,' 'keep' with 'endeavor to keep,' 'not use' with 'avoid using', and removing the obligation to destroy unused copies weakens the storage requirements and makes them discretionary. Also, removing words like 'physical and electronic location' makes it more ambigious.
perturbation.contradicted_law: French Law No. 78-17 of 6 January 1978 on Data Processing, Data Files and Individual Liberties (as amended)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 34 (and other articles) of the French Data Protection Act requires specific and diligent measures to protect personal data, including secure storage and access controls. The modified text's weakened language ('endeavor to keep,' 'avoid using,' 'precautions') conflicts with the law's requirement for concrete and auditable security measures, potentially leading to non-compliance regarding data protection and security.

Search Home Données personnelles – consultation publique sur le projet de recommandation relative aux mots de passe et autres secrets non partagés Données personnelles – consultation publique sur le projet de recommandation relative aux mots de passe et autres secrets non partagés 18 October 2021 La consultation nécessite la mise en œuvre d’un traitement de données à caractère personnel géré par la Commission nationale de l’informatique et des libertés. Print the article Email Decrease the size of the font Increase the size of the font Objet du traitement de données Le traitement a pour objet le recueil et l’analyse des retours effectués dans le cadre de la consultation publique. Il permet à la CNIL : de recueillir et conserver les contributions, en vue de leur analyse et de l’adoption de la recommandation modifiée ; le cas échéant, de contacter leurs auteurs pour approfondir les échanges et/ou pour les tenir informés des suites de la consultation ; d’établir des statistiques relatives aux contributions reçues. Base légale Article 6 (1) e du règlement général sur la protection des données – RGPD Ce traitement de données relève de l'exercice de l'autorité publique dont est investie la CNIL en application du règlement général sur la protection des données et de la loi Informatique et Libertés modifiée Données traitées Catégories de données traitées Données d’identification (nom, prénom, fonction) ; Coordonnées (adresse de courrier électronique) ; Nom de l’organisme ; Domaine d’expertise ; Contribution ; Date et heure de l’envoi de la contribution. Source des données Ces informations sont directement fournies par l’utilisateur du formulaire de consultation en ligne. Caractère obligatoire du recueil des données La participation volontaire à cette consultation nécessite, pour sa bonne gestion, le recueil de données permettant de savoir qui soumet une contribution et de contacter le contributeur en cas de besoin. Prise de décision automatisée Le traitement ne prévoit pas de prise de décision automatisée . Personnes concernées Le formulaire peut être utilisé par toute personne physique ou morale souhaitant faire part de ses observations ou propositions en lien avec la recommandation soumise. Destinataires des données les personnes en charge de la rédaction de la recommandation, en particulier les agents concernés du service de l’expertise technologique de la direction des technologies et de l’innovation et les agents concernés des services des contrôles de la direction de la protection des droits et des sanctions de la CNIL ; les personnes en charge de la mise en œuvre et du bon déroulement de la consultation, en particulier des agents du service de la communication interne et externe ; le cas échéant, les autres services de la CNIL concernés. Transferts des données hors UE Aucun transfert de donn
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the original's data protection obligations. The original text uses 'shall' indicating a mandatory obligation, while the modified text uses 'should' and 'endeavor to keep,' making the obligation discretionary. The law explanation states that the French Data Protection Act requires concrete and auditable security measures, which is undermined by the permissive language in the changed text. The scraped snippets do not directly support this contradiction, but the Law explanation sufficiently supports the determination of a contradiction, as 'should' and 'endeavor' are not concrete or auditable. This weakening of mandatory requirements to discretionary actions creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At the request of the supplier of the Proprietary Information the receiving party shall immediately return all Proprietary Information which the receiving party (or an Authorised Person/Agency Authorised Person) has in its possession, custody or control including all materials (in hardcopy or electronic form) that incorporate any part of the Proprietary Information and, if required, destroy any copies of such Proprietary Information and materials and certify in writing that such destruction has been completed.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request by the supplier of the Proprietary Information, the receiving party will use its best efforts to return Proprietary Information in its direct possession, and may, if practical, destroy copies.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall immediately return all' to 'will use its best efforts to return Proprietary Information in its direct possession, and may, if practical, destroy copies' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Reasonable request', 'best efforts', and 'if practical' introduce subjective criteria that could be used to avoid returning or destroying confidential information, and 'direct possession' is a less strict standard than 'possession, custody, or control'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article L122-6 of the French Commercial Code addresses protection of trade secrets and confidential information. While this specific article does not directly mandate immediate return, it lays the groundwork for enforcing confidentiality agreements. Weakening the obligation to return or destroy proprietary information makes it more difficult to enforce trade secret protection, and avoid being found in violation of general trade secret protections afforded in that jurisdiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: vue duquel elle est conçue, sans qu’il soit possible d’attribuer à chacun d’eux un droit distinct sur l’ensemble réalisé». Droit moral [ modifier | modifier le code ] Le droit moral regroupe : Le droit de divulgation ; Le droit de paternité ; Le droit au respect de l' intégrité de l'œuvre. L'application de ce droit est cependant nuancée dans la jurisprudence récente. Le droit de retrait et de repentir, qui consiste au retrait par l'auteur de son œuvre déjà divulguée de la sphère du marché en contrepartie d'une compensation financière à hauteur du préjudice subi par l'ayant droit, ou par le propriétaire du support (cas d'une peinture ou d'une sculpture notamment). Droits patrimoniaux [ modifier | modifier le code ] On distingue principalement : Le droit de reproduction ; Le droit de représentation. On peut également trouver d'autres droits patrimoniaux annexes, tels que le droit de traduction, le droit d'adaptation et le droit de destination. Il existe deux types de rémunération : une rémunération directe des auteurs qui consiste à obtenir des revenus directs, en général par le paiement des consommateurs (livres, CD…) ou par celui d'intermédiaires (achats de droits de télévision par les diffuseurs, part du chiffre d'affaires du diffuseur…) ; une rémunération indirecte qui consiste à s'assurer d'une remontée de revenus par divers mécanismes, par exemple à l'occasion de modification de reproductibilité (rémunération pour copie privée ), pour des utilisations qui ne permettent pas un contrôle unitaire des exploitations (barème des discothèques) ou pour des biens non-rivaux par nature (télévision et radio par la redevance ou la licence légale) ou encore par la rémunération au titre du prêt en bibliothèque . Cette rémunération se traduit en général par une absence de paiement direct par les consommateurs des œuvres ou des programmes. Les exceptions au droit d’auteur [ modifier | modifier le code ] Les exceptions à l'exercice du droit d'auteur sont fixées par l'article L.122-5 du code de la propriété intellectuelle [ 22 ] : Les principales utilisations que l’auteur ne peut interdire une fois l’œuvre divulguée sont : Les représentations privées et gratuites effectuées exclusivement dans un cercle de famille ; L'exception de copie privée , qui permet sous certaines conditions d'effectuer des copies ou reproductions strictement réservées à l'usage privé du copiste et non destinées à une utilisation collective. Sous réserve que soient indiqués clairement le nom de l'auteur et la source : Les analyses et courtes citations justifiées par le caractère critique, polémique, pédagogique, scientifique ou d'information de l'œuvre à laquelle elles sont incorporées ; Les revues de presse ; La diffusion, même intégrale, par la voie de presse ou de télédiffusion, à titre d'information d'actualité, des discours destinés au public prononcés dans les assemblées politiques, administratives, judiciaires ou académiques, ainsi que dans les réunions publiques d'ordre politique et les cérémonies officielles ; Les reproductions, intégrales ou partielles d'œuvres d'art graphiques ou plastiques destinées à figurer dans le catalogue d'une vente judiciaire effectuée en France pour les exemplaires mis à la disposition du public avant la vente dans le seul but de décrire les œuvres d'art mises en vente ; L'exception pédagogique, qui permet la représentation ou la reproduction d'extraits d'œuvres, à des fins exclusives d'illustration dans le cadre de l'enseignement et de la recherche, à l'exclusion de toute activité ludique ou récréative. Le public auquel cette représentation ou cette reproduction est destinée doit être composé majoritairement d'élèves, d'étudiants, d'enseignants ou de chercheurs directement concernés, et l'utilisation de cette représentation ou cette reproduction ne doit pas donner lieu à aucune exploitation commerciale. Enfin cette utilisation doit être compensée par une rémunération négociée sur une base forfaitaire sans préjudice de la cession du
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires immediate return of all proprietary information, while the changed version weakens this obligation to 'best efforts' and 'if practical', introducing subjective criteria. The law explanation suggests this weakening makes it more difficult to enforce trade secret protection, which is implicitly supported by the general principles in the French Commercial Code. The scraped law snippet discusses author's rights, specifically mentioning 'droit de reproduction' (right of reproduction) and 'droit de représentation' (right of representation), and exceptions to copyright. While not directly on point, it highlights the importance of protecting intellectual property rights. The modified text undermines the original, stronger obligation to protect the proprietary information, creating a situation where compliance with the original intent becomes less certain and weakens the ability to prevent unauthorized reproduction or representation of the proprietary information. This aligns with the 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition because the weakened obligation introduces ambiguity and makes the original intent difficult to enforce, which is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both the Agency and the Contractor shall promptly inform the other party if it is aware that any Proprietary Information supplied by the other party may have been accessed, used, distributed, circulated, supplied or copied in breach of this agreement and shall provide any assistance the other party may reasonably require to investigate 5such unauthorised access, use, distribution, circulation or copying and prevent further such unauthorised activities.
perturbation.changed_text: Both the Agency and the Contractor should, if feasible, inform the other party if it suspects Proprietary Information may have been inappropriately accessed, and consider providing assistance to investigate such access.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly inform' to 'should, if feasible, inform' and 'is aware that' to 'suspects' introduces a subjective element and weakens the obligation to report breaches. Replacing 'shall provide any assistance the other party may reasonably require to investigate' to 'consider providing assistance to investigate such access' makes providing assistance discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the contract states it is governed by French Law, the GDPR could also be applicable based on the processing of personal data. Article 33 of the GDPR requires controllers to notify data breaches to the relevant supervisory authority without undue delay, and where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it. The modified clause weakens the duty to inform and replaces awareness with suspicion, contradicting the strict and timely notification requirements of the GDPR in case personal data is involved, leaving the parties potentially non-compliant with EU law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 33 GDPR – Notification of a personal data breach to the supervisory authority - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority competent in accordance with Article 55 , unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. 2 Where the notification to the supervisory authority is not made within 72 hours, it shall be accompanied by reasons for the delay. The processor shall notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach. The notification referred to in paragraph 1 shall at least: describe the nature of the personal data breach including where possible, the categories and approximate number of data subjects concerned and the categories and approximate number of personal data records concerned; communicate the name and contact details of the data protection officer or other contact point where more information can be obtained; describe the likely consequences of the personal data breach; describe the measures taken or proposed to be taken by the controller to address the personal data breach, including, where appropriate, measures to mitigate its possible adverse effects. Where, and in so far as, it is not possible to provide the information at the same time, the information may be provided in phases without undue further delay. 1 The controller shall document any personal data breaches, comprising the facts relating to the personal data breach, its effects and the remedial action taken. 2 That documentation shall enable the supervisory authority to verify compliance with this Article. Suitable Recitals ( 85 ) Notification Obligation of Breaches to the Supervisory Authority ( 87 ) Promp
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to report data breaches, contradicting Article 33 of the GDPR, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2. The original text's 'shall promptly inform' is replaced with 'should, if feasible, inform,' and 'is aware that' becomes 'suspects,' making the reporting duty less stringent. The original text also requires assistance for investigations, which is changed to 'consider providing assistance,' making it discretionary. Article 33 of the GDPR requires controllers to notify data breaches to the supervisory authority without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. Therefore, the modifications introduce ambiguity and weaken the mandatory reporting requirements under GDPR.
